feed.title: I build World Models. Tumors break them.
feed.subtitle: We build generalizable, uncertainty-aware world models for tumor evolution and metastasis, models that (i) learn causal/functional links from genotype to regulatory state to phenotype, (ii) explicitly represent measurement noise and batch effects in real sequencing data, and (iii) transfer across cancers, tissues, and platforms. The scientific payoff is how somatic/germline variation rewires regulatory programs, how early genetic events bias invasive and metastatic routes, and how chromosomal instability (CIN) and tumor-intrinsic programs enable organ-specific colonization. The translational payoff is cheaper, scalable profiling and cleaner data, enabling robust biomarkers and therapeutic hypotheses.

entry.title: Pan-cancer inference and validation of hypermorphic, hypomorphic and neomorphic mutations

link.title: Pan-cancer inference and validation of hypermorphic, hypomorphic and neomorphic mutations

entry.content:
Mutations in cancer genes do not all behave the same way. While many are broadly classified as gain-of-function or loss-of-function, the functional consequences of mutations are often more nuanced.

In this work, we developed a computational framework to systematically infer hypermorphic, hypomorphic, and neomorphic mutations across diverse cancer types. By integrating large-scale genomic and transcriptomic datasets, we identified mutations that increase gene activity, partially reduce function, or generate entirely new molecular behavior.

The study combines computational prediction with experimental validation to demonstrate that these functional mutation classes can reshape regulatory networks in distinct ways. Our findings highlight the importance of moving beyond simple binary classifications of mutations and instead recognizing the diverse spectrum of functional effects that mutations can produce.

Understanding these mutation classes provides a more precise framework for interpreting cancer genomes and may help guide therapeutic strategies that target specific functional alterations.

Journal: Nature Genetics (2026)
entry.summary: Understanding functional mutation classes across cancers.

entry.title: Cellular states associated with metastatic organotropism and survival in pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma

link.title: Cellular states associated with metastatic organotropism and survival in pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma

entry.content:
Metastasis is responsible for the majority of cancer-related deaths, yet the biological programs that determine where tumors spread remain poorly understood.

In this study, we analyzed large-scale single-cell transcriptomic data from pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma (PDAC) to identify tumor cell states associated with metastatic organotropism. By integrating transcriptional programs with clinical outcomes, we uncovered cellular states that correlate with both metastatic patterns and patient survival.

These findings suggest that metastatic behavior is not solely determined by genetic alterations, but also by the transcriptional states adopted by tumor cells. Distinct cellular programs appear to predispose tumors to colonize specific organs and influence disease progression.

The work provides new insight into the relationship between tumor cell plasticity, metastatic dissemination, and clinical outcomes in PDAC.

Journal: Nature Genetics (2025)
entry.summary: Single-cell analysis reveals tumor programs linked to metastatic spread.

entry.title: Chromosomal instability and neural-like programs in NSCLC brain metastasis

link.title: Chromosomal instability and neural-like programs in NSCLC brain metastasis

entry.content:
This post highlights our recent work on non-small cell lung cancer brain metastasis.

Using multi-modal single-cell and spatial profiling, we found that brain metastases are characterized by pronounced chromosomal instability and neural-like transcriptional programs. Rather than simply reconstructing tumor evolution, this study focused on identifying biological features that distinguish metastatic tumors from primary lesions and may help explain metastatic fitness in the brain microenvironment.

Key themes from this work include:

  • chromosomal instability as a major feature of metastatic biology,
  • neural-like state programs in metastatic tumor cells,
  • integration of single-cell and spatial transcriptomic data,
  • implications for biomarker discovery and therapeutic targeting.

Citation: Tagore S, et al. PMID: 40016452
entry.summary: Highlights from our multi-modal single-cell and spatial study of brain metastasis.

entry.title: Welcome to my research blog
entry.content:
Welcome to my research blog.

I use this space to share brief updates on my work in cancer genomics, single-cell and spatial biology, and AI-based world models for tumor evolution and metastasis.

My research focuses on how genomic alterations reshape regulatory programs, how early events bias metastatic trajectories, and how uncertainty-aware computational models can improve biological discovery and translation.

Here I will post:

  • research updates,
  • method development notes,
  • publication highlights,
  • grant and project ideas,
  • and perspectives on computational oncology.

Thank you for visiting.
entry.summary: Research notes on cancer genomics, single-cell biology, and AI-driven modeling.
